title: Nigeria Is Quietly Becoming a Diplomatic Powerhouse
gist: Against the consensus view that Nigerian foreign policy has declined since Obasanjo, the author argues President Bola Tinubu's apparently chaotic diplomacy actually reflects a coherent, personally-rooted doctrine of defending liberal democracy in West Africa, evidenced by concrete interventions in Niger and Benin and a deepening U.S. counterterrorism partnership.

insights.claim: It has become axiomatic among scholars and analysts that Nigerian foreign policy has been on the decline for nearly two decades since former President Olusegun Obasanjo (1999–2007) restored the country's international standing after the military era.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: States the prevailing consensus the article sets out to overturn, useful baseline for judging the strength of the author's contrarian case.
insights.locator: The idea that Nigeria's foreign policy has been on the decline

insights.claim: Obasanjo's diplomatic strategy produced concrete results: $18 billion in debt relief, a surge in international investment, and Nigeria's establishment as the presumptive leader of the African continent.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: Sets the concrete historical bar of measurable diplomatic success against which Tinubu's more ambiguous record is being judged.
insights.locator: He did this all with brio

insights.claim: Every Nigerian president between Obasanjo and Tinubu failed to sustain a coherent foreign policy doctrine: Yar'Adua's health cut his term short, Jonathan burned through four foreign ministers in five years while battling Boko Haram, and Buhari's two full terms produced no diplomatic reset despite the opportunity.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: A two-decade pattern of foreign-ministry churn and drift is the backdrop making Tinubu's apparent chaos look, on the surface, like continuity rather than a break.
insights.locator: Goodluck Jonathan, who assumed power after Yar'Adua's unexpected death

insights.claim: Tinubu recalled all of Nigeria's career and noncareer ambassadors just three months into his presidency in September 2023, citing 'world-class efficiency,' then left many posts unfilled for months before abruptly naming three noncareer replacements in November, with many ambassadorial posts still empty as of this writing.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: A prolonged ambassadorial vacancy signals real institutional degradation in Nigeria's foreign service regardless of the author's broader contrarian thesis.
insights.locator: One of his first acts after becoming president

insights.claim: Tinubu logged approximately 261 days abroad across at least 30 countries on 51 unique and recurrent trips by June, per Nigerian newspaper the Punch, a travel intensity critics cite as evidence of directionless diplomacy rather than strategy.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: A concrete, citable travel-volume figure for benchmarking Tinubu's diplomatic activity level against peers.

themes.theme: The hidden doctrine: defending democracy in the neighborhood

insights.claim: The author's central thesis is that Tinubu's foreign policy is guided by a coherent, personally-rooted commitment to defending liberal democracy in Nigeria's immediate West African neighborhood, stemming from his own history as a pro-democracy activist forced into military-era exile.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: Reframes seemingly erratic Nigerian diplomacy as a legible pattern investors and policymakers can use to predict Tinubu's likely response to future West African coups or democratic crises.
insights.locator: Overall, Tinubu has been guided by a concern about safeguarding liberal democracy

insights.claim: Upon assuming ECOWAS's leadership in July 2023, Tinubu declared 'We will not accept coup after coup in West Africa again. Democracy is difficult to manage, but it is the best form of government,' setting an explicit anti-coup doctrine for the bloc.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: A direct public commitment gives a clear standard to measure Tinubu's subsequent actions against, and he has since followed through on it materially.
insights.quote: We must stand firm on democracy. There is no governance, freedom, and rule of law without democracy. We will not accept coup after coup in West Africa again.
insights.locator: Upon assuming the leadership of ECOWAS in July 2023

insights.claim: After Niger's July 2023 coup, Tinubu led ECOWAS in denouncing the putsch, issued a one-week ultimatum, and ordered electricity cut to Niger—Nigeria supplied about 70% of Niger's total electricity—not restoring it until the following March, though he ultimately failed to reinstate ousted President Bazoum.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: A quantified, high-leverage economic coercion tool (70% of a neighbor's electricity supply) demonstrates Nigeria has real material leverage over smaller West African states.
insights.locator: When Tchiani refused to back down

insights.claim: After a December coup attempt against Benin's democratically elected President Patrice Talon, Tinubu deployed Nigerian fighter jets and ground troops to help thwart the putschists, and has since sent additional troops aimed at deterring further threats and supporting a peaceful democratic transition.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: Direct military intervention to protect a neighboring elected government is a concrete, costly commitment that supports the doctrine thesis better than rhetoric alone.
insights.locator: In pursuit of that same principle, Tinubu has had greater success in Benin

insights.claim: Despite Burkina Faso, Mali, and Niger breaking away from ECOWAS in January 2025 to form the Alliance of Sahel States, Tinubu has initiated several meetings with them, especially Niger, aimed at tackling regional insurgency, reflecting a genuine desire to make ECOWAS whole rather than abandon the breakaway states.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: Ongoing outreach to junta states signals Nigeria is pursuing reintegration diplomacy rather than a purely punitive stance, a nuance that could matter for regional stability forecasts.
insights.locator: Tinubu has been severely tested by the antics of Burkina Faso, Mali, and Niger

insights.claim: Tinubu authorized direct U.S.-Nigeria military collaboration, including intelligence exchange and joint reconnaissance, despite political risk from northern Nigeria's Washington-skeptical political establishment; the resulting joint operation in May killed senior Islamic State commander Abu-Bilal al-Minuki.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: A concrete counterterrorism win (a named ISIS commander killed) is measurable evidence the U.S.-Nigeria security partnership is operationally active, not just declaratory.
insights.locator: Arguably the most important achievement of this collaboration

themes.theme: Nigeria still trails South Africa's assertiveness

insights.claim: Nigeria's diplomacy under Tinubu is still arguably playing catch-up to South Africa, which has positioned itself as the continent's 'moral conscience' through forthright actions like its landmark case against Israel at the International Court of Justice, while Nigeria continues to hedge its bets on controversial matters.
insights.speaker: Ebenezer Obadare
insights.so_what: Even under the author's favorable reading, Nigeria remains the more cautious, less publicly assertive African power relative to South Africa on contentious global issues.
insights.locator: To be sure, Tinubu is not Obasanjo
